tutorials.title: Rhino Basics
tutorials.description: Introduction to the Rhino 3D modeling interface, viewport navigation, and core tools.

steps.title: The Rhino Interface
steps.content:
## Getting Started with Rhino

When you first open Rhino, you'll see four viewports: **Top**, **Front**, **Right**, and **Perspective**.

### Key Interface Elements

- **Command Line** — At the top. Type commands here or use the menus.
- **Toolbars** — On the left and top. Commonly used tools for drawing, transforming, and analyzing geometry.
- **Properties Panel** — On the right. Shows details about selected objects.
- **Layer Panel** — Manage your drawing layers, colors, and visibility.

### Your First Steps

1. Open Rhino and select the **Small Objects — Millimeters** template
2. Try rotating the Perspective viewport by right-click dragging
3. Use the scroll wheel to zoom in and out
4. Middle-click drag to pan

steps.title: Viewport Navigation
steps.content:
## Navigating the Viewports

Rhino uses four standard viewports by default. Each shows a different projection of your 3D model.

### Navigation Controls

| Action | Mouse Control |
|--------|---------------|
| Orbit (Perspective) | Right-click + drag |
| Pan | Shift + Right-click + drag |
| Zoom | Scroll wheel |
| Zoom Extents | Double-click middle button |

### Viewport Types

- **Parallel projections** (Top, Front, Right) — No perspective distortion. Good for precise modeling.
- **Perspective projection** — Shows depth. Good for visualization.

### Tips

- Press `F6` to toggle the ground plane
- Use `Ctrl+Shift+E` to zoom extents in all viewports
- Double-click a viewport title to maximize it

steps.title: Drawing Basic Shapes
steps.content:
## Creating Geometry

Rhino offers many tools for creating 2D and 3D geometry. Start with the basics:

### 2D Shapes

- **Line** — Click two points to draw a line segment
- **Polyline** — Click multiple points to draw connected line segments
- **Circle** — Specify center and radius
- **Rectangle** — Click two opposite corners

### 3D Primitives

- **Box** — Create a rectangular box
- **Sphere** — Specify center and radius
- **Cylinder** — Base circle + height

### Object Snaps (Osnaps)

Enable Osnaps at the bottom toolbar to snap precisely to:
- **End** — endpoints of curves
- **Mid** — midpoints
- **Int** — intersections
- **Cen** — centers of circles/arcs

tutorials.title: Curves & Surfaces
tutorials.description: Learn to draw and edit NURBS curves, and understand the foundation of surface modeling.

steps.title: Understanding NURBS Curves
steps.content:
## NURBS Curves

NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines) are the mathematical representation Rhino uses for all geometry.

### Key Concepts

- **Control Points** — Points that influence the shape of the curve. The curve is attracted toward them but doesn't necessarily pass through them.
- **Degree** — Determines curve smoothness. Degree 1 = straight lines, Degree 3 = smooth curves.
- **Knots** — Internal parameters that affect how control points influence the curve.

### Drawing Curves

1. **InterpCrv** — Interpolated curve: passes through the points you click
2. **Curve** — Control point curve: points influence the shape
3. **Arc** — Circular arc through specified points

Try both `InterpCrv` and `Curve` to feel the difference.

steps.title: Editing Curves
steps.content:
## Editing Curves

### Turning on Control Points

Select a curve and press `F10` to show its control points. Press `F11` to hide them.

### Editing Operations

- **Move control points** — Click and drag individual points
- **Trim** — Cut a curve at intersection with another object
- **Split** — Divide a curve at a point
- **Join** — Combine end-to-end curves into a polycurve
- **Rebuild** — Redefine a curve with a new number of control points
- **Offset** — Create a parallel copy at a specified distance

### Best Practices

- Use fewer control points for smoother curves
- Check curve continuity with `GCon` command
- Use `CurvatureGraph` to visualize smoothness

tutorials.title: Surface Modeling
tutorials.description: Create and manipulate NURBS surfaces using lofts, sweeps, and other surfacing techniques.

steps.title: Surface Creation Methods
steps.content:
## Creating Surfaces

Surfaces in Rhino are created from curves. The main methods are:

### From Curves

- **Loft** — Creates a surface between two or more profile curves
- **Sweep1** — Sweeps a profile curve along one rail curve
- **Sweep2** — Sweeps a profile between two rail curves
- **NetworkSrf** — Creates a surface from a network of intersecting curves
- **Patch** — Fits a surface through a set of curves and points

### From Primitives

- **PlanarSrf** — Creates a flat surface from a closed planar curve
- **ExtrudeCrv** — Extrudes a curve in a direction to create a surface
- **Revolve** — Revolves a profile curve around an axis

### Tips

- Loft works best with curves that have the same number of control points
- Sweep1 is great for creating moldings and extrusions along a path

steps.title: Editing Surfaces
steps.content:
## Surface Editing

### Control Point Editing

Just like curves, surfaces have control points you can manipulate:

1. Select the surface
2. Press `F10` to turn on control points
3. Select and move individual points or rows of points

### Surface Operations

- **Trim** — Cut a surface using curves or other surfaces
- **Split** — Divide a surface without removing parts
- **Fillet** — Create rounded edges between surfaces
- **Blend** — Create smooth transitions between surface edges
- **Boolean operations** — Union, Difference, Intersection of closed solids

### Analyzing Surfaces

- **Zebra analysis** — Check surface continuity
- **Curvature analysis** — Visualize surface curvature
- **Draft angle** — Check moldability

tutorials.title: Solid Modeling
tutorials.description: Work with closed polysurfaces and boolean operations to create complex solid geometry.

steps.title: Solids in Rhino
steps.content:
## Solid Modeling

In Rhino, a **solid** is simply a closed polysurface — all edges are joined with no gaps.

### Creating Solids

- **Box, Sphere, Cylinder** — Built-in solid primitives
- **ExtrudeCrv** with `Cap=Yes` — Extrude a closed curve into a solid
- **Join** — Join individual surfaces into a closed polysurface
- **Cap** — Close open ends of a polysurface

### Boolean Operations

- **BooleanUnion** — Combine two solids into one
- **BooleanDifference** — Subtract one solid from another
- **BooleanIntersection** — Keep only the overlapping volume

### Checking Solidity

Use `ShowEdges` > `Naked Edges` to find gaps in your polysurface. All edges must be joined for a valid solid.

tutorials.title: Intro to Grasshopper
tutorials.description: Get started with Grasshopper's visual programming interface for parametric design.

steps.title: What is Grasshopper?
steps.content:
## Grasshopper: Visual Programming for Design

Grasshopper is a visual programming environment that runs inside Rhino. Instead of writing code, you connect **components** (nodes) with **wires** to build algorithms.

### Why Grasshopper?

- **Parametric design** — Change an input and the entire design updates
- **Repetitive tasks** — Automate patterns, arrays, and transformations
- **Exploration** — Quickly test design variations
- **Data-driven design** — Import data to drive geometry

### Opening Grasshopper

In Rhino, type `Grasshopper` in the command line or click the Grasshopper icon.

### The Canvas

- **Components** live on the canvas. Drag them from the toolbar tabs.
- **Wires** connect outputs (right side) to inputs (left side)
- Data flows **left to right**

steps.title: Your First Definition
steps.content:
## Building a Simple Definition

Let's create a parametric circle grid:

### Steps

1. Place a **Number Slider** (Params > Input > Number Slider)
2. Place a **Series** component (Sets > Sequence > Series)
3. Connect the slider to the Series **Count** input
4. Place a **Point** component (use Construct Point)
5. Connect the Series output to the **X** input
6. Place a **Circle** component (Curve > Primitive > Circle CNR)
7. Connect the points to the Circle **Center** input

Now move the slider — the number of circles changes!

### Key Concepts

- **Green** components = all inputs connected, geometry generated
- **Orange** components = missing inputs or warnings
- **Red** components = errors

tutorials.title: Data Trees
tutorials.description: Understand Grasshopper's data tree structure — the key to mastering complex definitions.

steps.title: Understanding Data Trees
steps.content:
## Data Trees

Grasshopper organizes data in **trees** — hierarchical structures with branches and items.

### Tree Anatomy

- **Path** — The address of a branch, e.g. `{0;1;2}`
- **Branch** — A list of items at a given path
- **Item** — A single piece of data (number, point, curve, etc.)

### Why Trees?

When you perform an operation on a grid of points, the result naturally organizes into rows (branches) of points (items).

### Visualizing Trees

- **Param Viewer** — Shows the tree structure as text
- **Panel** — Displays all data with path labels
- Right-click a wire and select **Draw Fancy Wires** to see data flow

### Common Operations

- **Flatten** — Collapse all branches into one list
- **Graft** — Put each item in its own branch
- **Simplify** — Remove unnecessary path levels

tutorials.title: Essential Components
tutorials.description: A tour of the most useful Grasshopper components for architectural modeling.

steps.title: Geometry Components
steps.content:
## Key Components for Architecture

### Curves
- **Polyline** — Connect points into a polyline
- **Interpolate** — Smooth curve through points
- **Offset Curve** — Offset in a plane

### Surfaces
- **Loft** — Surface between curves
- **Extrude** — Push a curve along a vector
- **Boundary Surface** — Fill a closed curve

### Transformations
- **Move** — Translate geometry by a vector
- **Rotate** — Rotate around an axis
- **Scale** — Uniform or non-uniform scaling
- **Mirror** — Reflect across a plane
- **Orient** — Map from one plane to another

### Lists
- **List Item** — Get item by index
- **Dispatch** — Split a list by a pattern
- **Cull Pattern** — Remove items by a boolean pattern

tutorials.title: GH Scripting
tutorials.description: Write custom C# and Python scripts inside Grasshopper components.

steps.title: Scripting in Grasshopper
steps.content:
## Custom Scripts in Grasshopper

When built-in components aren't enough, you can write custom logic.

### C# Script Component

1. Place a **C# Script** component (Maths > Script > C# Script)
2. Double-click to open the editor
3. Define inputs/outputs by right-clicking the component
4. Write your logic in the `RunScript` method

### Python Script Component

1. Place a **GhPython** component (Maths > Script > GhPython)
2. Double-click to open the editor
3. Use `rhinoscriptsyntax` or `RhinoCommon` libraries

### When to Script?

- Complex math or logic that's hard to wire visually
- Text processing and file I/O
- Custom data structures
- Interfacing with external APIs or libraries

tutorials.title: Python Basics
tutorials.description: Learn Python fundamentals: variables, loops, functions, and data structures.

steps.title: Python Fundamentals
steps.content:
## Python for Designers

Python is a versatile programming language that's easy to learn and widely used in design computation.

### Variables and Types

```python
name = "Architecture"    # string
floors = 12              # integer
height = 3.5             # float
is_tall = True           # boolean
```

### Lists

```python
points = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
mixed = ["hello", 42, True]
```

### Loops

```python
for i in range(10):
    print(i)

for point in points:
    print(point)
```

### Functions

```python
def calculate_area(width, height):
    return width * height

area = calculate_area(5, 10)  # 50
```

steps.title: Data Structures
steps.content:
## Working with Data

### Dictionaries

```python
building = {
    "name": "Tower A",
    "floors": 20,
    "height": 80.0
}

print(building["name"])  # Tower A
```

### List Comprehensions

```python
# Create a list of squares
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]

# Filter even numbers
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]
```

### File I/O

```python
# Read a CSV
import csv
with open('data.csv') as f:
    reader = csv.reader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row)
```

tutorials.title: Python in Rhino
tutorials.description: Use RhinoScriptSyntax and RhinoCommon to automate Rhino with Python scripts.

steps.title: RhinoScriptSyntax
steps.content:
## Python Scripting in Rhino

Rhino includes a built-in Python editor. Open it with `EditPythonScript`.

### RhinoScriptSyntax

The `rhinoscriptsyntax` library provides simple functions for common operations:

```python
import rhinoscriptsyntax as rs

# Draw a point
rs.AddPoint(0, 0, 0)

# Draw a line
rs.AddLine([0,0,0], [10,0,0])

# Draw a circle
rs.AddCircle([0,0,0], 5)

# Get user input
pt = rs.GetPoint("Pick a point")
if pt:
    rs.AddSphere(pt, 2)
```

### Automating Tasks

```python
import rhinoscriptsyntax as rs

# Create a grid of spheres
for x in range(5):
    for y in range(5):
        rs.AddSphere([x*3, y*3, 0], 1)
```

tutorials.title: Python in Grasshopper
tutorials.description: Use the GhPython component to write Python scripts that integrate with Grasshopper data flows.

steps.title: GhPython Component
steps.content:
## Python in Grasshopper

The GhPython component lets you write Python inside Grasshopper definitions.

### Setup

1. Place a **GhPython** component on the canvas
2. Right-click the inputs to rename and set type hints
3. Double-click to open the code editor

### Example: Custom Point Grid

```python
import Rhino.Geometry as rg

points = []
for i in range(count_x):
    for j in range(count_y):
        pt = rg.Point3d(i * spacing, j * spacing, 0)
        points.append(pt)

a = points  # 'a' is the output
```

### Working with Data Trees

```python
from Grasshopper import DataTree
from Grasshopper.Kernel.Data import GH_Path

tree = DataTree[object]()
for i in range(rows):
    path = GH_Path(i)
    for j in range(cols):
        tree.Add(rg.Point3d(i, j, 0), path)

a = tree
```

tutorials.title: Laser Cutting
tutorials.description: Prepare files for laser cutting: line weights, kerf compensation, and material settings.

steps.title: Preparing Laser Cut Files
steps.content:
## Laser Cutting Basics

### File Preparation

Laser cutters read vector files. Prepare your geometry in Rhino:

1. **All curves must be planar** (flat on the XY plane)
2. **Use layers for cut types:**
   - Red = cut through
   - Blue = score/etch
   - Black = raster engrave
3. **Line weights matter** — Hairline for cuts, thicker for engraving
4. **Export as DXF or AI** format

### Kerf Compensation

The laser beam has width (~0.1-0.3mm). For tight-fitting joints:
- Offset **cut lines inward** by half the kerf width
- Test with a small sample first

### Material Settings

Every material needs specific **speed** and **power** settings:
- Cardboard: High speed, low power
- Acrylic: Medium speed, high power
- Plywood: Low speed, high power

Always run a test cut on scrap material first.

tutorials.title: 3D Printing
tutorials.description: Export meshes for 3D printing, understand supports, and optimize print quality.

steps.title: 3D Printing from Rhino
steps.content:
## Preparing Models for 3D Printing

### Requirements

- Model must be a **closed solid** (watertight mesh)
- Use `ShowEdges > Naked Edges` to check for gaps
- Minimum wall thickness depends on material (typically 1-2mm)

### Export Workflow

1. **Check** your model is a valid solid
2. **Mesh** — Use `Mesh` command with custom settings for desired resolution
3. **Export as STL** — File > Export Selected > STL
4. **Open in slicer** (Cura, PrusaSlicer, etc.)

### Key Slicer Settings

- **Layer height** — 0.1mm (fine) to 0.3mm (fast)
- **Infill** — 15-20% for most architectural models
- **Supports** — Enable for overhangs > 45 degrees
- **Brim/raft** — Improves bed adhesion for small contact areas

### Tips

- Orient the model to minimize supports
- Scale matters — check units before exporting
- Hollow large models to save material and time

tutorials.title: CNC Milling
tutorials.description: Prepare 2D and 3D toolpaths for CNC routing, including feeds, speeds, and material setup.

steps.title: CNC Basics
steps.content:
## CNC Milling for Architecture

### What is CNC?

CNC (Computer Numerical Control) routers use rotating bits to cut material. Unlike laser cutting, CNC can create 3D topography.

### Types of Operations

- **2D Profiling** — Cut outlines through sheet material
- **2D Pocketing** — Carve recessed areas to a set depth
- **3D Surfacing** — Mill complex 3D surfaces with ball-end bits

### File Preparation

1. Model your geometry in Rhino at **full scale**
2. Organize layers: cut profiles, pockets, 3D surfaces
3. Export to CAM software (RhinoCAM, Fusion 360, Mastercam)

### Key Parameters

- **Bit diameter** — Determines minimum feature size and corner radius
- **Feed rate** — How fast the bit moves (material dependent)
- **Spindle speed** — RPM of the cutting bit
- **Step-down** — Depth per pass (never cut full depth in one pass)
- **Step-over** — Distance between parallel toolpath passes

trails.description: A guided path from Rhino fundamentals to parametric design with Grasshopper.

trails.title: Computational Design
trails.description: Learn to code for design — from Python basics to scripting in Grasshopper.

trails.description: From screen to shop — prepare your designs for laser cutting, 3D printing, and CNC.
